name: Rich Chicken Stock
recipeIngredient: 7 1/2 lb Whole chicken and parts, such as backs, feet, wings
recipeInstructions:
Stock is the foundation of all good cooking. It is so important that it deserves emphasis. Good stock, especially chicken stock, is essential for the cuisine of Hong Kong, whose subtle flavors, fresh ingredients, and fast cook techniques require very good stock. Light, flavorful, and versatile chicken stock should be considered a staple, alongside salt, cooking oil, or soy sauce.
While commercially prepared canned or cube stocks are available, many are of inferior quality, being too salty or containing additives and colorings that negatively affect health and the natural taste of food. Making your own stock is best. You can make a large batch and freeze it for later use. Here are some guidelines for making good stock:
Use about 50 percent bones and 50 percent meat. Without meat, the stock will lack body, richness, or depth of flavor and may taste watery. Stewing hens are ideal because they are inexpensive and full of flavor.
Simmer, never let the stock come to a boil as that results in a cloudy, heavy stock with less flavorful and digestible liquid.
Use a tall, heavy pot so the liquid covers all solids and evaporation is slow.
Simmer on low heat and gently skim the stock every now and then to remove impurities.
Strain the stock slowly through several layers of cheesecloth or a fine mesh strainer.
Allow the stock to cool thoroughly before storing in the refrigerator or freezer.
Saving uncooked chicken bones and carcasses will provide essential ingredients for stock quickly and economically.
The stock should be rich and full-bodied, achieved by simmering it for an extended period. This long simmering allows flavors to develop and results in a flavorful stock that yields delicious sauces, providing true Hong Kong taste.
Cut the chicken into pieces and place them with the parts into a large pot. Cover with cold water and bring to a simmer. Using a large, flat spoon, gently skim off any scum that rises from the bones. Do not stir or disturb the stock. Skim until the stock is clear, which may take 30 to 40 minutes.
Reduce heat to a low simmer and add ginger, scallions, and salt. Simmer for at least 3 hours, skimming fat off the top at least twice during this time. Strain through several layers of dampened cheesecloth or a fine mesh strainer, then allow it to cool. Remove any fat that has risen to the top. The stock is now ready for use or can be transferred to containers and frozen for future use.
Makes 6 quarts.
